title: REST API Basics: Resources, HTTP Verbs, and Status Codes
excerpt: A practical beginner-friendly guide to REST APIs: what resources are, how GET/POST/PUT/DELETE work, and which HTTP status codes matter most.

value.title: What a REST API actually is

body.value:
A REST API is a way for systems to communicate over HTTP using clearly defined endpoints.

In practice, it lets one application ask another application for data or request that something be created, updated, or removed.

The most important idea is this: the API exposes resources, and clients interact with those resources using standard HTTP methods.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: Think of a REST API as a contract for working with business resources through HTTP.

body.value:
A resource is usually a business object such as a user, order, invoice, or product.

Endpoints should be named around those resources, not around actions.

  • /api/users
  • /api/orders
  • /api/products

This is more scalable and easier to understand than action-based naming.

• GET - read data
  • POST - create new data
  • PUT - replace existing data
  • PATCH - partially update existing data
  • DELETE - remove data

Even when the back-end is complex, the external API should keep this behavior predictable.

value.caption: Basic REST interaction
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|GET /api/orders| B[REST API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B -->|200 OK + JSON| A

body.value:
Status codes are part of the API contract. They tell the client what happened.

  • 200 OK - request succeeded
  • 201 Created - new resource created
  • 204 No Content - success with no response body
  • 400 Bad Request - invalid input
  • 401 Unauthorized - authentication required
  • 403 Forbidden - authenticated but not allowed
  • 404 Not Found - resource does not exist
  • 500 Internal Server Error - unexpected server failure

value.title: Practical tip
value.text: Use status codes consistently. Clients should not need to guess whether an operation succeeded.

body.value:
Most REST APIs exchange data as JSON because it is lightweight, readable, and supported almost everywhere.

A typical response might return a collection, a single resource, metadata, or validation errors.

Good APIs use plural nouns, stable URLs, and clear resource hierarchy.

  • /api/customers
  • /api/customers/15/orders

Avoid names like /getOrders or /createUser because HTTP verbs already describe the action.

value.title: Simple controller example in ASP.NET Core

value.caption: Minimal REST-style controller example

body.value:
A good REST API is built around resources, uses HTTP methods consistently, returns meaningful status codes, and keeps naming predictable.

Once these basics are clear, topics like authentication, versioning, pagination, and API security become much easier to understand.

title: Designing RESTful Web APIs — A Practical Guide from Fundamentals to Production
excerpt: Learn how to design RESTful APIs that scale, evolve, and remain maintainable. Covers HTTP fundamentals, resources, verbs, paging, caching, versioning, and security.

value.title: Why API Design Comes Before Implementation

body.value:
One of the biggest mistakes developers make is starting to build an API before designing it. Unlike internal code, APIs become contracts. Once clients depend on them, changing them becomes difficult or even impossible.

A well-designed API is long-lasting, predictable, and easy to evolve. This is why design must come first — not code.

REST APIs are built on top of HTTP, so understanding HTTP is essential. Every request consists of:

  • Verb (what to do)
  • Headers (metadata)
  • Body (data)

Every response contains:

  • Status code
  • Headers
  • Body

value.caption: Basic HTTP request-response flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client -->|Request: GET /customers| Server
Server -->|Response: 200 OK + JSON| Client

body.value:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style based on:

  • Client-server separation
  • Stateless communication
  • Cacheable responses
  • Uniform interface

In practice, REST is about designing intuitive, predictable APIs — not strictly following theory.

In REST, everything is a resource. Resources are represented via URIs:

  • /customers → collection
  • /customers/1 → single resource

Query strings should be used only for optional behavior like filtering or paging.

REST uses HTTP verbs to describe actions:

  • GET → retrieve
  • POST → create
  • PUT → update (full)
  • PATCH → update (partial)
  • DELETE → remove

value.caption: REST operations on resources
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Collection[/customers/] -->|POST| NewCustomer
Collection -->|GET| ListCustomers
Item[/customers/{id}/] -->|GET| GetCustomer
Item -->|PUT| UpdateCustomer
Item -->|DELETE| DeleteCustomer

body.value:
Idempotency means repeating the same request produces the same result.

  • GET, PUT, DELETE → idempotent
  • POST → NOT idempotent

This is critical for reliability in distributed systems.

Responses should be:

  • Consistent
  • Technology-agnostic
  • Client-friendly

Use JSON conventions like camelCase and avoid leaking database structure.

REST APIs should express relationships via URIs:

  • /customers/1/invoices
  • /projects/22/tickets

This makes APIs intuitive and self-explanatory.

value.caption: Resource relationships
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Customer --> Invoices
Customer --> Projects
Project --> Tickets

body.value:
Never return unbounded collections. Always support paging.

Use query strings like ?page=1&pageSize=25.

Errors should be meaningful and structured.

Use status codes + error body:

Use HTTP caching to reduce load.

ETags allow clients to ask: “Has this changed?”

value.caption: ETag caching flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Server: GET /resource
Server-->>Client: 200 OK + ETag
Client->>Server: GET + If-None-Match
Server-->>Client: 304 Not Modified

body.value:
APIs must evolve without breaking clients.

  • Query string: ?v=1
  • Header: X-Version
  • Content type: application/vnd.api.v1+json

Consistency is more important than the method you choose.

Security must be part of design, not an afterthought.

  • Use HTTPS
  • Implement authentication
  • Apply authorization rules

value.caption: Token authentication flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Server: Login (credentials)
Server-->>Client: JWT Token
Client->>Server: Request + Token
Server-->>Client: Response

body.value:
• Design APIs before implementing
  • Think in resources, not database tables
  • Use HTTP correctly (verbs, status codes)
  • Plan for paging, caching, and versioning
  • Secure your API from day one

Great APIs are not accidental — they are designed.
